domains.basis: Intent and knowledge: means to engage in conduct or is aware consequence will occur ordinarily.
domains.caution: Rome Statute crimes may include special mental elements outside Article 30.
domains.label: International criminal law Article 30

domains.basis: Purpose, knowledge, recklessness, negligence; intent inferred from acts and circumstances.
domains.caution: Distinguish motive from intent and read the governing statute.
domains.label: Common law / MPC mens rea

domains.basis: Direct or indirect intent, conditional intent, and negligence framed through codes.
domains.caution: Code text controls; dolus eventualis treatment varies by jurisdiction.
domains.label: Civil-law dolus / culpa

domains.basis: Guilty intention/criminal responsibility with strict proof rules in some offense categories.
domains.caution: Evidentiary rules vary strongly by offense class and jurisdiction.
domains.label: Islamic-law criminal responsibility

domains.basis: Responsibility inferred through harm, relational breach, warnings, repair, and community context.
domains.caution: This is a comparative abstraction, not one unified formal code.
domains.label: Restorative/customary responsibility

cautious_language: This lattice compares semantic support for intent-like mental elements across selected legal traditions. The inverse criminality space marks adverse-inference darkness, not guilt. Real legal outcomes depend on the jurisdiction, offense, evidence rules, burden of proof, and fact finder.
